column24:
In my Databases class, we were tasked with building a comprehensive database design of our choosing. Instead of starting with one large diagram, my team chose to split the design into smaller focused Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams that represented separate parts of the system. By then connecting these smaller diagrams into a complete ER diagram, we were able to ensure that no important details were missing, allowing us to communicate ideas clearly. 
As I worked to successfully merge the diagrams, I helped improve the system by creating a clear, efficient database structure that made it easier for analysts, developers, and end-users to access and manage the theoretical data.
I learned that designing these ER Diagrams is a crucial first step that transforms data from communicated words into a format ready for analysis. ER diagrams act like translators, connecting human language and machine readable data, sitting right at the intersection of data, systems and people. 
Looking ahead, I see ER diagrams and thoughtful database design as essential tools for smoothing collaboration and minimizing errors during database creation and use. Good data modeling empowers teams to make better decisions by making data accessible and aligned with business needs. I’m excited to apply this mindset to improve processes and systems that deliver real value to users and clients.

column24:
The signature summit of my high school’s Inquiry and Innovation (i2) program is the i2 Showcase – bringing together current students to present their projects and prospective students and their families to learn about the program. As the inaugural i2 Editor-in-Chief during my junior year of high school, I was tasked with organizing this signature event, and I hoped to improve the experience for both groups by making presentation setup more efficient for students and project viewing more intuitive for the audience.

I accomplished my goal by developing a data collection form for project teams, allowing me to collect data on project type, team size, topic areas, and presentation needs (including posters, outlets, and AV equipment). With that information, I manually developed a room layout system in a spreadsheet. I grouped projects with similar themes together, mixed larger and smaller teams to balance room capacities, and ensured each room had accurate supply counts.

While I primarily completed the task manually, I began to understand the impact and importance of even the most basic systems and sorting algorithms, allowing efficient coordination and organization to reduce friction and improve overall experiences. This role, and this task specifically, taught me the importance of designing scalable and modular systems that serve people’s needs, something I hope to develop on the Internal Insights team at Generate.

column24:
During my co-op, I noticed that the weekly enrollment report caused confusion. The data came from fragmented data sources like dense Tableau tables with little context, which led to delays in enrollment planning. To address this, I proposed creating an automated RMarkdown report that would highlight key metrics through clear visuals and simple explanations. 

After approval, I reached out to different teams to understand their concerns and goals, then tailored the visualizations based on their requests. For example, I created confidence intervals broken down by program to highlight enrollment predictions, deposits, and other components. I then did walkthroughs to explain the report, so technical and non-technical teams could use it effectively. As a result, enrollment planning finished one month earlier. 

This experience taught me the value of designing with the end-user in mind. It wasn't just about compiling the most important data points, but also about building a data product that’s comprehensible for everyone, regardless of their familiarity with the data. Going through this process pushed me to take initiative and build my communication skills by engaging with different teams, asking targeted questions, and continuously adjusting the report based on feedback. Through this experience, I can now say I feel more confident in my communication skills and ability to design data tools tailored for my end-users.
column25:
To improve the lived experience of Generate members, I would focus on inclusion, communication, and workload balance through these three initiatives: 

First, I would introduce a mentorship pairing system to support new members during onboarding. It can feel overwhelming to join an established team and not know where to start or how to contribute. So pairing new members with returning members can give them someone to check in with, ask questions, and build early connections. This would reflect Generate's value of inclusivity by helping new members feel more confident, included, and ready to engage meaningfully with their team. 

Second, I'd implement a bi-weekly anonymous check-in survey to gather feedback across teams. The responses could be viewed both holistically and by team to identify specific concerns. The quick surveys would focus on areas like team dynamics, workload, and sense of belonging. For example, “How supported did you feel by your team this week?” The goal is to spot trends in real time, so leadership can better understand how members are feeling and take action based on concerns that are highlighted.  

Finally, with so many ongoing projects, it’s easy for members to become overloaded, especially near deadlines. I’d introduce a workload mapping tool (Clockify or AirTable) where members log their weekly tasks, estimate time spent, and tag task difficulty. This would help teams spot patterns. For example, if someone consistently takes on high-effort work or if a member needs more support on a task. This visibility can help project leads better support their members and balance workload.

I see this role as a way to grow more confident navigating those challenges by learning how to approach open-ended problems, collaborate across teams, and sharpen my data analysis skills to uncover inefficiencies and drive meaningful improvements. 

At ClaimCrisis, I started tracking internal progress to create structure, but considering the team is small, the data was limited. I want to challenge myself to design scalable tracking systems in a multi-team environment like Generate to learn how to create impactful data tools.

Finally, I haven't had the chance through my co-op or clubs to work closely with a larger team of like minded peers in my field. I see this role as a unique opportunity to be part of a data-driven team, where I can learn from others by collaborating and observing how they approach problem solving and structure their analysis to drive impact.

column24: Being a member of my high school mock trial team was a life-changing experience that grew my abilities in public speaking, political literacy, and self-confidence. column24:
Last year, as Head of Finance for the Minis Committee (part of the Asian Student Union), I helped manage our mentorship program. We spent two weeks matching hundreds of mentor and mentee applications based on hobbies, majors, and preferences to create meaningful connections for first and second-year students joining our Boston campus community.

When some mentees didn't connect with their assigned groups, I created the "Re-pairings program" – a second chance for students to switch groups. Initially, we resisted group changes to encourage "unexpected friendships.” But I discovered that some circumstances were special, so I moved forward with implementing this new system, but with a few caveats. To prevent casual group-hopping, I required a written explanation and a one-month commitment to the original group before allowing changes.

Through this experience, I discovered that listening to feedback and being willing to adapt are crucial. Sometimes mistakes happen in the initial process, but that's okay as long as you're responsive to the community you serve. It was through studying previous years' feedback, personal conversations with members, and end-of-year surveys we'd collected that I realized our role isn't to force relationships that aren't working, but to ensure members have a positive experience in the program.
column25: First, I’ve wanted to address that I fully support Google Forms and surveys as they are quick and easy, but often, I’ve found that most club members don't respond or don’t go the lengths to give detailed feedback. I've found two more effective approaches to this problem. First, provide a compelling incentive by offering a $5 Starbucks gift card in exchange for a 20-minute sit-down conversation. This encourages more genuine, detailed responses through a relaxed, verbal format, and for the respondent, no typing is required. Second, keep it simple. Have each person write one thing they enjoyed and one thing they disliked on a post-it note during the biggest event of the semester. Execute this at a high-attendance event like the Generate Showcase with one crucial detail: ask for feedback before they enter the room. Towards the end of the event, audience members are typically tired and want to exit the room as quickly as possible. While these methods may seem dull, they offer solid practicality with a hint of creativity to gather meaningful feedback.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23: I haven’t been part of Generate before, but I’m drawn to how it creates space for students to collaborate across disciplines while staying grounded in their own. I’m especially interested in the Internal Insights Team because I like working behind the scenes to make things clearer and smoother for everyone else. I appreciate that this role isn’t just about organizing feedback, but about helping teams reflect, grow, and function better together. I’ve done this in labs, in clubs I’ve helped run, and even while tutoring and mentoring, any time I can take something messy and make it more useful, I’m in. Generate feels like a space where intellect and creativity comes together. I’d love to contribute to that and learn from it too.
column24: As the founder of Blue Crew, my school’s marine biology and conservation club, I organized a mangrove plantation event where we planted over 100 saplings. While the environmental impact was our focus, I realized how much behind-the-scenes structure it took to make the event meaningful and manageable for everyone involved. While planning, I realized we didn’t have a clear process for tracking who was doing what, volunteers were signing up in group chats, and tasks were being repeated or missed entirely. To make things smoother, I created a structured volunteer signup form and a shared task list that outlined roles, timing, and materials needed. I also coordinated with our partner organization to make sure logistics like transportation and sapling delivery were confirmed in advance. I also shared a pre-event guide so volunteers would know what to expect on the day. I also collected post-event feedback to see room for improvement; some volunteers were unsure about where to go or had issues with transportation timing. That showed me that even a well-organized plan needs flexibility and more real-time communication. Using feedback and reflection to create clarity and reduce stress is the kind of mindset that excites me about this role.
column25: I’d implement anonymous check-ins every few weeks to create a safe space for members to share honestly, with prompts that focus on specific areas like workload, sense of community, inclusion and personal growth. I think another idea could be to have a digital anonymous suggestion box. Alongside this, I would regularly share accessible resources and workshops on stress management and mental health based on feedback from check-ins. I also think it’s important to celebrate progress to help everyone feel valued and seen. Maybe we could highlight one member each month to showcase their contributions and what they’ve brought to Generate. It’s a simple way to recognize people’s efforts and boost motivation.
column26: What I really want to develop in this role is practical experience in automating routine tasks and making internal operations more efficient. I’ve seen how repetitive tasks can slow things down in clubs I’ve helped run, and I want to get better at identifying those bottlenecks and fixing them with real tech solutions. I’m excited to learn how to use software and workflows to save time and improve communication for the benefit of the team and organization. Getting better at this kind of behind-the-scenes problem-solving feels like a skill that would not only help Generate but also my future work wherever I go.

column16: Events Team (first semester), Eatwell Client Team (last semester)
column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23:
Being on the first ever data client team, I know first hand how  complex and unpredictable working with a client can be. Yet despite all the obstacles the team and I faced during the semester, we managed to deliver a product we were proud of. I learned how to collaborate with a stakeholder, adapt to limitations (like working solely with Google Sheets), and quickly pivot/troubleshoot (like switching to Google Sheets API). Despite all that, showing up in the corner room of the Sherm after a long day of co-op and still being able to laugh -even while pumping out a few more lines of code - was one of the most rewarding parts of my week. 

Two semesters later, I’m still extremely close to those who I’ve formed connections with on my time as an Events coordinator, and even more so the amazing people who I worked with on Eatwell. Even then, I loved meeting people in other branches/fields that I normally never get to see as a data science major. Generate played a pivotal role in my third year, and I’m eager to continue building those meaningful experiences into my fourth year.
column24: During my internship at the Institute for Health Equity and Social Justice Research under Dr. Laura Senier, I helped lead a CBPR-focused study with MFFC and GreenRoots, investigating how limited access to green and blue spaces in Chelsea and Mattapan affects teen mental health. We organized and facilitated community events, conducted youth exit interviews, and discovered key themes-transportation, cost, distance, and discrimination-all of which restrict their access to enriching environments. To improve how findings were mobilized, I began sharing insights weekly with community partners, using visual slide decks to pinpoint specific factors and recommendations. This shared understanding led to more targeted initiatives and informed a grant proposal to city officials advocating for expanded transit access, discounted youth programming, and safer indoor/outdoor spaces. By translating raw interview data into clear visuals and stories, I helped transform lived experiences into actionable strategy. What I learned is that strong data communication is an essential component of any initiative - storytelling that is grounded in evidence and visualization empowers narratives in ways raw numbers alone cannot. It made our findings more relatable and persuasive for partners and funders, and helped our grant proposal clearly convey both urgency and impact.
column25: I think a really fun idea would be to have Generate members work on another team’s project for a day, or shadow them live. I’ve always been curious about how other teams, like hardware, approach their work and structure their processes. I think that experiencing different workflows firsthand would be a great way to foster mutual understanding and spark new ideas. It would be similar to All Hands, except much more involved. Even during All Hands, I felt that I talked to a lot of new members and learned a lot that I normally wouldn’t have myself. It would be a engaging way to not only learn unique things you wouldn’t typically learn in your own discipline, but understand the types of challenges and types of creativity that your fellow Generate friend goes through. As a previous member, I’ve always felt that certain branches were distant from others, which is why I think implementing a plan like this would help foster more genuine connection and moments of authenticity.
column26:

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23:
I’ve always known friends and classmates who were part of Generate, and while I was initially intimidated by how established and skilled the community seemed, learning more about it showed me that it’s much more than just a traditional tech club; it’s a collaborative space built on learning and passion. I was drawn to that strong sense of community and the opportunity to apply skills beyond the classroom.

The Internal Data Analyst position especially interests me because it blends two areas I care deeply about: community building and data. Over the past two years, I’ve been involved in a cultural organization where I helped organize events, gather feedback, and implement changes,  all aimed at improving community experiences. I’ve seen firsthand how valuable thoughtful iteration and data-driven decisions can be in creating meaningful engagement. Academically, I’ve developed a strong foundation in data science, working with Python and learning how to turn data into actionable insights. Being able to apply these skills to support and improve Generate’s internal operations and member experience would be an amazing opportunity, both to give back and to keep growing.
column24:
During my recent co-op at a large accounting firm in the commercial department, I had the opportunity to improve a set of internal forms and processes used by our team. These forms were used for client intake and records, but they were complicated, inconsistent, and took too long to complete.

I was able to analyze the existing process, identify redundancies, and redesign the forms to be more streamlined, user-friendly, and adaptable across different clients. To ensure the changes would actually help the team, I gathered input from coworkers in different positions and incorporated their feedback into the final version. The biggest lesson I learned was how differently people approach processes, even when they use the same tools. What seems efficient to one person might be confusing to another. Taking time to understand those varying perspectives helped me design a solution that worked for everyone, not just for me. It showed me how thoughtful collaboration can turn a good system into a great one.
column25:
If given full freedom, I would focus on creating in-person conversations to better understand the lived experiences of Generate members. I envision hosting a community-wide event with small breakout groups, each led by a community leader and composed of 5–10 members from the same team. Each group would work through a curated set of discussion questions aimed at uncovering what’s working, what could improve, and what members hope to see from Generate in the future.

Community leaders would take notes on key themes and insights from their groups, then regroup afterward to identify common patterns across teams. These insights could then inform specific changes to improve the member experience.

I believe this kind of peer-to-peer dialogue surfaces richer, more nuanced feedback than a general form. In-person discussions allow people to build off each other’s ideas, share personal experiences, and highlight concerns that might otherwise go unspoken.
column26: Through this role, I hope to further develop my skills in community building, by applying what I’ve learned and gaining more hands-on experience in a space I truly care about. I’m also excited by the challenge of stepping outside my comfort zone. This position allows me to combine two of my core interests: working with people and engaging with data. I see it as a unique opportunity to grow both personally and professionally while contributing to a community.

column21: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it.
column23:
When I think about what I want most out of my college experience, it’s not just the roles I take on, but the memories and people I share them with. That’s what draws me to Generate. I’ve heard so much about how strong the community is, not just professionally, but socially. The idea of being part of a group where I can sit down for lunch with people who get excited about the same things I do, show up to events together, and build real friendships while working on meaningful projects. That’s exactly the environment I want to be in. 

I’m specifically drawn to the Internal Data Analyst role because it lives at the intersection of strategy, systems, and community. I’m excited by the chance to dive into data that reflects how Generate runs, and to help make internal decisions more informed, efficient, and human-centered. It’s a way to learn from a tight-knit team while contributing in a meaningful, behind-the-scenes way.
column24:
Over the summer, I worked with the International Transformation Foundation, a nonprofit operating in Kenya that was still tracking most of its field work manually on paper. I partnered with their team to design and implement an Airtable-based dashboard system that digitized their data collection, streamlined internal workflows, and enabled easier tracking of key metrics. This tool supported over 20 staff members in managing their programs more efficiently, helping them make better decisions and reduce time spent on repetitive administrative work. 

Through that experience, I learned that improving a system starts with understanding the people who use it. Before jumping into development, I spent time talking with staff about their day-to-day challenges, how they used their data, and what “better” actually looked like for them. That taught me the value of designing with and not just for others. It also showed me that the best systems aren’t necessarily the most complex, instead, they’re the ones that make people’s work feel easier, clearer, and more aligned with their goals.
column25:
Improving lived experience starts with understanding that data isn’t just numbers, instead it’s people’s stories, frustrations, and moments of joy translated into patterns. To approach this challenge, I’d blend both qualitative and quantitative methods to get a full picture of what it feels like to be a Generate member. 

I’d begin by listening. That means holding space for open-ended conversations, running surveys with thoughtful, non-generic questions, and facilitating small focus groups that represent different teams, roles, and backgrounds. I’d want to dig into not just what’s going well or what needs fixing, but how members feel at different stages of their Generate journey from onboarding to project wrap-up to social integration. Are people feeling connected? Overwhelmed? Inspired? 

From there, I’d pair that feedback with internal data, things like project feedback forms, event attendance, or participation rates. I’d look for patterns, blind spots, or key transition moments that might need more support. 

Finally, I’d design targeted, lightweight experiments such as new rituals, clearer onboarding timelines, member recognition tools, or improved feedback systems and track their impact. Importantly, I’d keep it iterative. Member experience is always evolving, and I’d want our solutions to evolve alongside it, grounded in both empathy and evidence.
column26:
More than anything, I hope to deepen my ability to use data not just for insight, but for empathy. I’ve had experience building systems, organizing information, and analyzing performance, but this role gives me a chance to connect that work more directly to people’s lived experiences. I want to learn how to craft internal processes that are not only functional, but human-centered designed to reduce friction, build clarity, and help teams thrive. 

I also want to grow as a strategic communicator. I’m excited by the challenge of taking complex or messy data and translating it into actionable, accessible stories that others can rally around. Whether it's helping leadership see the bigger picture or empowering a teammate to solve a specific problem, I want to get better at turning numbers into narratives that matter. 

And lastly, I want to learn from the people around me. Generate’s culture of intentionality, kindness, and shared purpose is something I deeply admire. I want to contribute to that energy while also absorbing everything I can from how teams run effective meetings to how they support each other during crunch time. I see this as a chance to grow both technically and personally, and I look forward to the opportunity to start!

column23: I’m excited to explore how Generate’s community and entrepreneurial spirit drive product development. What draws me to Generate is its mission to not only build innovative products, but also to cultivate the growth of its members. I’m passionate about the internal data analyst role because I want to grow the organization by improving internal processes and communication. column24: During my operations internship at CPC, I identified an opportunity to streamline a repetitive task that was consuming valuable time for project managers. At the time, they had to manually document project requests by copying notes from a Smartsheet into a PDF document. This process was both time-consuming and prone to error. To improve this, I first took time to fully understand the process and the needs of the project managers. I identified pain points and researched tools that could integrate seamlessly with Smartsheet. My research led me to the Smartsheet Document Builder feature which allowed us to automate the creation of these request letters directly from the sheet, eliminating the need for manual data entry. Through this experience, I learned the importance of thoroughly understanding business requirements before finalizing a solution. It’s not just about automating a task, but about creating a process and system that is scalable and adaptable for all users. I also saw how small process improvements can have a large impact on team efficiency.
column25: I would create a centralized and continuous feedback system to better understand the experience of Generate members. This would include feedback forms available after events and throughout the semester. Members could share concerns and suggestions in the forms, with prompts regarding friction points such as time commitments, team dynamics, onboarding, and workload. To gain insights from this feedback, I would create a centralized database in a platform like Excel to collect and organize responses. The data would then be regularly reviewed to identify trends or pain points such as client deadlines and engagement. These findings could directly impact next steps for process improvements like updating onboarding materials and adjusting events. I would include a monthly summary showing the changes we have made based on the feedback received. Ultimately, this feedback system would create a welcoming environment where Generate members feel comfortable sharing their experiences, allowing us to continuously iterate and improve member experiences.
column26: I hope to deepen my understanding of how efficient operations and strong support systems can make communities more connected and resilient.
